query: neonatal jaundice maternal anti-B antibodies mechanism

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In Rh incompatibility, fetal blood carries paternal Rh-positive antigens and is perceived by the maternal immune system of an Rh-negative mother as foreign. Maternal antibodies are produced, particularly anti-D antibodies. Subsequent pregnancies with Rh-positive fetuses can lead to increased antibody production, as the mother's immune system retains memory of the antigen. A similar mechanism operates in ABO incompatibility, where maternal antibodies target fetal RBCs carrying A or B antigens not present in maternal blood. This immune response can result in severe complications for the fetus or neonate, including anemia, jaundice, hydrops fetalis, or kernicterus. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Etiology -- Increased Bilirubin Production
retrieved_docs.content: In Rh incompatibility, an Rh-negative mother, exposed to Rh-positive RBCs during a previous pregnancy, becomes sensitized and develops antibodies against Rh antigen. Initially, sensitization produces IgM antibodies that cannot cross the placenta. However, during subsequent pregnancies, IgG antibodies cross the placenta, causing RBC hemolysis in a fetus with Rh-positive blood. The Rh antigen is very immunogenic, and the resultant HDN is usually severe, often leading to hydrops fetalis or severe UHB in newborns. Prevention of neonatal UHB caused by immune-mediated hemolysis begins in pregnancy with the recognition of mothers at risk for developing Rh antibodies. The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) recommends that all Rh-negative pregnant women receive anti-D immune globulin at 28 weeks gestation and again after delivery if the infant is Rh-positive or the blood type is unknown. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Etiology -- Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Miscellaneous : Other conditions that may cause CHD include idiopathic neonatal hepatitis, parenteral nutrition-induced cholestasis, gestational alloimmune liver disease, neonatal hemochromatosis, and hypotension. Parenteral nutrition-associated cholestasis (PNAC) is a significant iatrogenic cause of cholestasis in preterm infants managed with parenteral nutrition (PN). PNAC is present in about 20% of neonates receiving PN for 2 weeks or longer. [56] Duration of PN use and intestinal failure are 2 independent risk factors for PNAC. Though the mechanism is not entirely clear, likely being multifactorial, abnormal bile salt metabolism due to prematurity and the harmful effects of PN components are thought to be the main culprits. [57] Other factors, including sepsis and necrotizing enterocolitis, can also potentiate liver injury. [58] Gestational alloimmune liver disease (GALD), which causes almost all cases of neonatal hemochromatosis, is a fulminant alloimmune disorder and results from intrahepatic and extrahepatic iron deposition, leading to liver failure. [59] In GALD, maternal IgG immunoglobulin against fetal hepatocytes crosses the placenta, causing complement-mediated damage to fetal hepatocytes. Characteristic features involve signs of liver failure, including hypoglycemia, coagulopathy, hypoalbuminemia, cholestatic jaundice, edema, and elevated liver enzymes. GALD has a risk of recurrence in subsequent pregnancies of approximately 90% and can result in fetal or neonatal death. [60] The term idiopathic neonatal hepatitis is used when the etiology of neonatal cholestasis cannot be ascertained after an extensive diagnostic evaluation. Newer diagnostic tools enable more precise diagnoses, with fewer cases of neonatal cholestasis now classified as "idiopathic." [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Rh Hemolytic Disease(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, no effects are seen in first pregnancies for Rh-D-mediated disease, as IgM is a large pentamer that can not cross the placental barrier. However, during the following pregnancies, subsequent exposure of as little as 0.03mL of Rh-positive cells can lead to the formation of anti-D IgG immunoglobulins, which cross the placenta freely and bind to fetal red blood cells containing D-surface antigen. These antibody-coated cells are recognized by the fetal reticuloendothelial system, and the destruction of these cells causes the release of large amounts of bilirubin in fetal circulation. During the antenatal period, maternal conjugation enzymes remove the excess bilirubin, but after birth, due to early insufficiency of glucuronyltransferase enzymatic activity, neonates present with jaundice or kernicterus and severe hemolytic anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Etiology -- Increased Bilirubin Production
retrieved_docs.content: The production of bilirubin can increase secondary to immune-mediated hemolysis caused by blood group incompatibilities (eg, such as ABO and Rhesus (Rh) incompatibility) and nonimmune mediated hemolysis, which is caused by RBC membrane defects (eg, hereditary spherocytosis and elliptocytosis), RBC enzyme defects (eg, glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase [G6PD], pyruvate kinase deficiencies), sequestration-like cephalohematoma, subgaleal hemorrhage, intracranial hemorrhage, polycythemia, and sepsis. Exaggerated immune or nonimmune mediated hemolysis is the most common cause of pathologic hyperbilirubinemia. Immune-mediated hemolysis occurs with blood group incompatibility as ABO/Rh incompatibility and causes hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN). In HDN from ABO incompatibility, preformed maternal anti-A and anti-B antibodies of the immunoglobulin G (IgG) subclass cross the placenta and cause hemolysis and UHB in newborns with blood types A, B, or AB. Although the direct antiglobulin test (DAT) aids diagnosis, the sensitivity and positive predictive value for severe UHB are low. [9] ABO incompatibility between mother and fetus exists in about 15% of pregnancies, but HDN occurs in only about 4% of newborns with ABO incompatibility. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Etiology -- Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Infection :  Congenital infections (eg, syphilis, toxoplasmosis, HIV, herpes virus, and rubella) should be included in the differential diagnosis of neonatal cholestasis, especially when stigmata of congenital infection, including growth restriction, coagulopathy, skin rash, or thrombocytopenia, are present. Cytomegalovirus (CMV) is also a common congenital infection with many manifestations. Most infected newborns are asymptomatic, but hepatomegaly and CHB may indicate hepatic involvement. [36] Carefully reviewing maternal history, specific serologies, and viral culture results aid diagnosis. Urine and blood cultures are also a component of the diagnostic evaluation, as urinary tract infections and septicemia can cause CHB in neonates. Additionally, microcirculatory changes in the liver, a direct effect of bacterial products, and toxins released by bacteria are thought to be the possible mechanisms of cholestasis in patients with UTI. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonimmune Hydrops Fetalis -- Etiology -- Immune Hydrops Fetalis
retrieved_docs.content: Immune hydrops fetalis is primarily caused by red blood cell alloimmunization due to Rh or ABO incompatibility. In Rh incompatibility, the fetal blood carries paternal Rh-positive antigens, which are recognized as foreign by the immune system of an Rh-negative mother, leading to the production of maternal anti-D antibodies. In subsequent pregnancies with Rh-positive fetuses, these antibodies can be produced in larger quantities as the maternal immune system retains a memory of the antigen. A similar mechanism occurs in ABO incompatibility, where maternal antibodies target fetal red blood cells carrying A or B antigens not present in the maternal blood. This immune response can lead to severe fetal or neonatal complications, including anemia, jaundice, hydrops fetalis, or kernicterus. However, with the introduction of anti-D immune globulin, immune hydrops fetalis has become rare. [9] [10] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn -- Pathophysiology -- ABO-Induced Hemolytic Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Inherent ABO incompatibility, which occurs in 15% to 25% of pregnancies, is another pathophysiologic mechanism that can cause HDFN. [16] Only about 1% of those pairs, those with high IgG titers, will develop HDFN due to ABO incompatibility. [16] The ABO system comprises the blood types A, B, AB, and O. Individuals produce anti-A or anti-B antibodies depending on the antigens not present in their RBCs at ages as young as 3 months, through exposure to these foreign antigens in various foods and bacteria. Consequently, ABO-induced HDFN can occur even during the first pregnancy, mainly in women with an O blood type and a fetus with an A, B, or AB blood type. [1] Compared with Rh-induced HDFN, ABO incompatibility generally causes a less severe HDFN. Studies have posited that this may be due to fetal RBCs expressing fewer ABO blood group antigens than adults or that ABO blood group antigens are expressed by many tissues, reducing the chance that antibodies specifically target the antigens on fetal RBCs. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Pathophysiology -- Alloimmune Hemolytic Anemia
retrieved_docs.content: In hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn, maternal sensitization in an RhD-negative individual occurs due to previous exposure to the Rh antigen, either through transfusion with Rh-positive RBCs or pregnancy with an Rh-positive offspring. Consequently, Rh hemolytic disease of the newborn generally does not occur in the first pregnancy in the absence of a transfusion history. Immunologically, antibody secretion begins with IgM, which cannot cross the placental barrier. This is later followed by isotype switching, leading to the production of IgG antibodies. IgG antibodies can cross the placental barrier, and they do so during the second and subsequent pregnancies, attacking the fetal RBCs and causing hemolysis and associated complications such as hydrops fetalis and jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: Rho(D) Immune Globulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism by which RhIG prevents isoimmunization is not completely understood but is likely to suppress the immune response and antibody formation in Rh-incompatible individuals (Rh-negative exposed to Rh-positive RBCs). If administration occurs within 72 hours of full-term delivery, the incidence decreases from 12% or 13% to 1% or 2%. The chances of isoimmunization drop to less than 1% with the administration schedule at 28 weeks gestation and within 72-hours postpartum. [2] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn -- Pathophysiology -- Rh-Induced Hemolytic Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, as fetal RBCs are broken down, excess bilirubin enters fetal circulation, which the placenta in utero removes. However, at birth, the fetal liver must begin processing the bilirubin. Indirect bilirubin, or unconjugated bilirubin, is conjugated to direct bilirubin using the enzyme uridine diphosphate-glucuronosyltransferase. This conjugated bilirubin is excreted in bile and ultimately expelled through feces and urine. In infants, especially preterm infants, liver processing is less efficient, which often leads to natural physiologic jaundice. [14] With excess breakdown products from HDFN, these immature processing mechanisms are overwhelmed, and the resulting hyperbilirubinemia can be prominent. The accumulation of unconjugated bilirubin can lead to kernicterus, a neurologic condition that develops as the unbound bilirubin crosses the blood-brain barrier and deposits in the brain and central nervous system of the developing newborn. [15] Prompt recognition and treatment of hyperbilirubinemia and HDFN are paramount to avoiding long-term neurologic dysfunction in these infants.

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Hypogammaglobulinemia of Infancy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Transient hypogammaglobinemia of infancy may represent an exaggerated physiologic nadir when maternal IgG begins to decrease and before the child begins to synthesize their own immunoglobulin.  Although the mechanism is not fully elucidated, the presence of maternal immunosuppressive antibodies (IgG) crossing the placenta, that disrupt the humoral immune system of the neonate is one suggestion. This inhibition can cause a reduced level of not only IgG but also IgM and IgA, as seen in some cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Evaluation -- Diagnosis Studies in Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Recommended studies to identify a hemolytic disease etiology of unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia include maternal and neonatal blood types, a direct antibody test (DAT), complete blood cell (CBC), reticulocyte count, blood smear, and G6PD testing. Serum albumin, considered a surrogate marker for free bilirubin, should be checked, especially if the TSB level approaches the level where exchange transfusion is indicated. Free bilirubin is the fraction responsible for bilirubin-induced toxicity. [92] The bilirubin-albumin ratio (B/A) ratio is an additional tool that may predict the risk of kernicterus and serve as a guide when considering the escalation of care and exchange transfusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Etiology -- Miscellaneous Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Other miscellaneous causes of UHB include infants of mothers with diabetes (IDM), gastrointestinal obstruction, congenital hypothyroidism, and certain medications. IDM often have polycythemia, with an increased incidence of jaundice. [27] UHB in congenital hypothyroidism is related to decreased bilirubin hepatic uptake, impaired UGT activity, and sluggish gut motility, while gastrointestinal obstruction promotes increased bilirubin recycling by augmenting enterohepatic circulation. When prescribed in the neonatal period, certain medications may worsen UHB by displacing bilirubin from albumin, affecting albumin binding. [28] Sepsis can also predispose a newborn to UHB by causing oxidative damage to RBCs and increasing bilirubin load. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are clinical entities that commonly arise during the fetal and/or neonatal period and can continue throughout infancy, a stage that lies within the broader period of life between birth and death. These conditions are also known by the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي,” reflecting their cross‑linguistic recognition. Laboratory assessment frequently includes measurement of total hemoglobin concentration, and results that fall below the reference range are interpreted as supportive of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice. The disorders are closely related to, and may be synonymous with, Rh alloimmunization (تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh), underscoring an immuno‑hematologic mechanism of red‑cell destruction. Recognizing the timing of occurrence, the significance of low hemoglobin values, and the link to Rh alloimmunization is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective management of affected newborns and infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified as a specific form of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN. The broader category of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN encompasses several distinct types of neonatal jaundice, including Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, the general condition يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis in the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth). These interrelated entities reflect different pathophysiological mechanisms—such as hemolysis, immature hepatic conjugation capacity, and prematurity—that can contribute to hemorrhagic and hemolytic disease in the newborn. Recognizing how Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome fits within this spectrum and how the various forms of neonatal jaundice relate to the overarching disease category is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management of affected infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 597
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified as a form of Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. Within this broader category, several specific types of neonatal jaundice are recognized. Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation is listed as a child condition of Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn, indicating that impaired bilirubin conjugation can accompany the hemorrhagic and hemolytic processes in the newborn period. The Arabic term يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice) is also identified as a child of Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn, reflecting the general occurrence of jaundice in this clinical context. Additional subtypes include يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by epidermal immaturity in fetuses or newborns), which highlights jaundice that arises when the skin barrier is underdeveloped, and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (premature‑birth‑associated neonatal jaundice), emphasizing the heightened risk of jaundice in infants born preterm. Together, these relationships illustrate that Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome and its associated neonatal hemorrhagic and hemolytic disease encompass a spectrum of jaundice presentations, each linked to distinct pathophysiological mechanisms such as delayed bilirubin conjugation, immature skin barrier function, and prematurity, all of which are important considerations for diagnosis and management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 150
retrieved_docs.content: (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان); and * Jaundice specifically caused by Rh alloimmunization in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). These jaundice subtypes are often evaluated together with total hemoglobin concentration, where values below the reference range support the diagnosis of hemolytic disease. Recognizing the interrelated nature of neonatal infections, genetic syndromes, hemolytic processes, diverse jaundice mechanisms and hemorrhagic events—along with appropriate laboratory interpretation—is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely intervention and optimal care of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1200
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to a spectrum of neonatal jaundice conditions and related complications. It is associated with neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation, as well as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic (newborn) jaundice, and jaundice that is specifically related to preterm birth. In addition, this condition can be accompanied by a loose umbilical cord clamp that leads to bleeding. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations is important for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2344
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is categorized within the broader condition of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn encompasses several related pediatric entities, including neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth). These interconnected disorders illustrate the diverse mechanisms—such as impaired bilirubin conjugation, respiratory tract bleeding, milk‑related bilirubin excess, and prematurity‑related metabolic immaturity—that can contribute to hemorrhagic and hemolytic presentations in the newborn, underscoring the need for comprehensive evaluation and targeted management in this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a neonatal hemorrhagic and hemolytic disease spectrum that includes specific syndromes (Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, Ingkikte‑galsyndroom) and conditions such as bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified). This overarching condition, termed NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN, comprises multiple bleeding complications—including losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding (umbilical cord clamp loosening with bleeding), نزف سري بعد الولادة (post‑natal umbilical bleeding), and نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory‑tract bleeding)—and a variety of jaundice phenotypes: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, neonatal jaundice due to delayed development of the conjugating system, يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), general يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (jaundice caused by hemolysis in the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (physiologic jaundice associated with preterm birth). These entities illustrate how hemorrhagic, hemolytic, and conjugation‑defect mechanisms interrelate in newborns, emphasizing the need for integrated recognition, assessment of underlying cause and gestational age, and coordinated management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1199
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is identified as a neonatal condition, indicating that it manifests during the newborn period. The neonatal qualifier encompasses several distinct clinical entities. Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system represents a form of early‑life hyperbilirubinemia caused by the immature hepatic capacity to process bilirubin. In addition, neonatal hemorrhage after birth (نزف سري بعد الولادة) and neonatal respiratory tract bleeding (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ) are recognized as bleeding complications that can occur in the immediate post‑natal period, affecting either the umbilical region or the respiratory passages. Another neonatal manifestation is nuclear jaundice resulting from the protective coating (التمنيع الإسوي) in embryos or newborns (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), which reflects a specific type of jaundice linked to fetal or neonatal physiological factors. Together, these entities illustrate the range of disorders and complications that are characteristic of the neonatal stage, emphasizing the importance of early recognition and management of metabolic, hemorrhagic, and respiratory conditions in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: and perinatal jaundice are evaluated by total hemoglobin concentration, and laboratory values below reference ranges support their diagnosis. Neonatal jaundice manifests in multiple specific forms: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), nuclear jaundice (يرقان نووي), nuclear jaundice caused by epidermal immaturity in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), physiologic neonatal jaundice (يرقان وليدي) and physiologic neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). These jaundice types arise from hemolysis, delayed bilirubin conjugation, nutritional factors (breast‑milk) or birth‑timing complications, and they frequently co‑occur with hemorrhagic events. Hemorrhagic disorders anchored in the Neonatal period include loss of umbilical cord clamp leading to bleeding

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2328
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are conditions that typically arise during the fetal and/or neonatal period. They are closely linked to the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي,” which denotes neonatal jaundice, indicating that these entities represent the same clinical phenomenon of jaundice occurring in newborns. The condition is also related and possibly synonymous with “تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh,” suggesting an association with Rh‑mediated hemolytic disease of the newborn. In the laboratory context, measurements of total hemoglobin concentration are interpreted in light of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice; when hemoglobin values fall below the reference range, this finding is considered an important interpretive clue for the presence of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice. Recognizing these interconnections aids clinicians in diagnosing and managing jaundice in the newborn period, especially when Rh alloimmunization is a contributing factor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1206
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified), Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation (including the specific form caused by delayed development of the conjugating system), نزيف حديثي الولادة (neonatal hemorrhage), and يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice) are all conditions that occur during the neonatal period. The qualifier “Neonatal” therefore applies to each of these entities, indicating that their onset, presentation, or occurrence is confined to the first weeks of life. Recognizing that these diverse disorders share the common temporal context of the neonatal stage helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and management strategies appropriate for newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.content: This hemolytic process is a central component of the broader entity NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN, which also includes various hemorrhagic complications such as umbilical cord bleeding from a loose clamp (losgeraakte navelstrengklem; نزف سري بعد الولادة), post‑natal umbilical hemorrhage, respiratory‑tract hemorrhage (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ), and intracranial neonatal hemorrhage (نزيف مخي وليدي). Multiple forms of neonatal jaundice are captured within this umbrella: * Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation of bilirubin and the related “delayed development of conjugating system,” reflecting immature hepatic enzyme activity; * Physiologic newborn jaundice (يرقان نووي); * Breast‑milk‑induced jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي); * Prematurity‑related jaundice (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان); and * Jaundice specifically caused by Rh alloimmunization in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). These jaundice

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: neonatal period, anchors a spectrum of early‑life disorders such as hereditary metabolic disease (Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome), multiple jaundice subtypes (neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation of bilirubin, breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic neonatal jaundice, hemolytic jaundice, perinatal jaundice, Rh‑alloimmunization‑related jaundice, prematurity‑associated jaundice), and hemorrhagic presentations (general neonatal hemorrhage, intracranial neonatal hemorrhage, umbilical bleeding after birth, respiratory‑tract bleeding). These hemorrhagic events are linked to an unspecified perinatal condition that may arise from perinatal infections, which include unspecified bacterial sepsis of the newborn and neonatal botulism. Laboratory findings described as “Below reference range” are interpreted in the context of hemolytic and perinatal jaundice, providing quantitative guidance for diagnosis. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2140
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteriële sepsis van pasgeborene, niet gespecificeerd is a neonatal condition that can occur in newborns. The neonatal (qualifier value) is associated with several neonatal disorders, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (post‑umbilical bleeding after birth), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (physiologic jaundice caused by immature bilirubin conjugation in fetuses or newborns), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑related jaundice). These interrelated conditions illustrate the spectrum of neonatal complications that may present in the early post‑natal period, ranging from infectious sepsis to various forms of jaundice, and underscore the importance of recognizing neonatal qualifiers in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 150
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal period, encompassing the fetal and post‑natal stages from birth onward, is the clinical context for a spectrum of infectious, genetic, hematologic and hepatic disorders that frequently overlap in timing, pathophysiology and laboratory findings. Bacterial sepsis of the newborn is a common neonatal infection, while genetic conditions such as Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome and Ingedikte‑galsyndroom are recognized as child manifestations of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN, predisposing infants to severe hemolysis, bleeding and jaundice. Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are closely linked to Rh alloimmunization (تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي), in which maternal anti‑Rh antibodies destroy fetal red blood cells, producing profound neonatal hemolysis and hyperbilirubinemia. This hemolytic process is a central component of the broader entity NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN, which also includes various hemorrhagic complications such as

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 284
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are medical conditions that most frequently appear during the fetal and/or neonatal period and are especially prevalent in infancy, a specific stage within the broader period of life between birth and death. These conditions are synonymous with the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي” (neonatal jaundice) and are closely linked to Rh immunoprophylaxis (“تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh”), reflecting the role of Rh incompatibility in their pathogenesis. Laboratory evaluation of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice relies on the measurement of total hemoglobin concentration and the red blood cell count (“عدد خلايا الدم الحمراء”), which are used to interpret the severity of the condition. When such test results fall below the reference range, this is interpreted as an indicator of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice. Although the condition can be observed throughout the lifespan, its primary occurrence is in the early stages of life, underscoring the importance of early detection and appropriate management in newborns and infants.
